# Questions in Livestream 23

Live Stream #23: The 23 Enigma

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=257" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:17</a> | If Egypt influenced the Old Testament so much, why is so much more focus placed on Mesopotamia? | He said the imbalance mostly comes from academic tradition and language training, since Mesopotamian studies are easier to add onto Hebrew-based biblical study than Egyptology is. | He explains the imbalance as a feature of scholarly training rather than evidence that Egypt is unimportant. Biblical scholars more often learn Semitic languages and can move toward Akkadian or Ugaritic more easily than into Egyptian, so Mesopotamian comparisons dominate the field. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=445" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:25</a> | What does he know about the Egyptian papyri Joseph Smith owned and "translated"? | He said the surviving papyri are ordinary funerary texts from the Book of the Dead tradition, not Abraham's writings. | He identifies the extant Joseph Smith papyri as standard Egyptian funerary material rather than a hidden Abrahamic text. The surviving content belongs to the Book of the Dead and related traditions, so it does not support Smith's claimed translation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=663" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:03</a> | Is there evidence that Ramesses II lost his firstborn son and that Egyptian activity in the Levant declined around the Exodus? | Yes to both in a broad sense. He dated the firstborn's death to roughly Ramesses II's year 25 and said Egyptian hegemony later waned. | He connects two broad observations without claiming a direct Egyptian confession of the Exodus. Ramesses II's firstborn appears to have died around year 25, and Egypt's later control in the Levant weakened, which he sees as compatible with an Exodus-era disruption. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=837" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:57</a> | What does he think of the Talpiot tomb claim as Jesus's final resting place? | He rejected it, saying the names are too common and the case is statistically and methodologically weak. | He rejects the Talpiot argument because its name cluster is not distinctive enough to identify Jesus's family. In his view, the statistical framing and historical method are weak, so the tomb does not overturn the resurrection or burial traditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=1006" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:46</a> | Why should Adam and his sons be read as clans or tribes when Genesis gives them a narrative? | He said Genesis is structured around clan politics, and the genealogies make better sense when the named fathers stand for clan lines rather than isolated individuals. | He argues that narrative form does not prevent names from representing larger kin groups. Genesis repeatedly uses fathers, sons, and genealogies to explain peoples and clan relationships, so Adam's line can be read in that representative framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=1341" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:21</a> | Why is the abandonment of Avaris considered important evidence for the Exodus by some scholars? | Because the site shifts abruptly from settlement use to funerary use in the Ramesside period, raising the question of where its Levantine population went. | He treats Avaris as significant because the archaeology shows a major change in use. A settlement with a large Asiatic or Levantine population becomes associated with burials instead, which creates a historical question that Exodus models try to explain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=1590" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:30</a> | Does he accept systems-collapse theory for the Bronze Age collapse, and were the Sea Peoples a cause or a symptom? | Partly. He said collapse unfolded over centuries, with the Sea Peoples acting as both symptom and contributing cause. | He accepts a multi-causal collapse model but resists treating the Sea Peoples as the single explanation. The collapse involved long-term stresses, and the Sea Peoples both reflected those disruptions and intensified them in places they attacked. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=1811" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:11</a> | Were the Nephilim a fallen priesthood, and what about the Anakim? | He said the Nephilim are better understood as the result of a fallen priesthood, while he deferred a fuller answer on the Anakim. | He prefers a fallen-priesthood interpretation over angel-human offspring for the Nephilim question. He does not fully develop the Anakim in this answer, leaving that part more tentative and less complete than his Nephilim response. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=1999" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:19</a> | Is ancient idolatry basically like a genie-in-a-lamp view of trapping a god in an image? | Fairly close. He said idols were often treated as images meant to entice or host divine presence, and Genesis 1 reverses that logic by making humans God's image. | He says the analogy works if understood carefully: idols were not always thought to be the god itself, but an image prepared to receive or mediate divine presence. Genesis reverses that religious logic by presenting living humans as God's authorized image. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=2180" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:20</a> | Does Tubal-cain working iron argue against reading Genesis 4 in clan terms? | No. He said naming an individual craft founder still fits a clan-based reading because clans are named from their progenitors. | He says craft-founder language is compatible with clan representation. A named ancestor can stand for the origin of a trade or lineage, so Tubal-cain's association with metalworking does not require a strictly individualistic reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=2482" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:22</a> | Should Behemoth and Leviathan be identified as a hippo and a crocodile, and how should archangels be viewed in ancient Near Eastern context? | He rejected a simple animal reading for Leviathan, treating it as a mythic sea monster and reading biblical angelic language through intertextual engagement with older imagery. | He allows that animal imagery may be involved, but he rejects reducing Leviathan to a crocodile. He reads Leviathan as mythic chaos-monster imagery and treats angelic or archangelic language as biblical engagement with ancient Near Eastern divine-council and messenger categories. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=2763" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:03</a> | Is there evidence of witchcraft, wizardry, or magic elsewhere in the ancient Near East the way scripture describes? | Yes, in abundance. He said Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the Levant are saturated with magical practice, with the key distinctions often being public versus private and legitimate versus illegitimate use. | He says ancient Near Eastern cultures provide abundant evidence for magic, divination, and ritual specialists. The important distinction is not whether magic existed, but whether a practice was public, priestly, state-approved, private, illicit, or necromantic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=2942" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:02</a> | What is "abrech" in Genesis 41:43? Does it mean something like "kneel" or have an Egyptian origin? | He said the term is still unclear. An Egyptian etymology is possible, but he was not prepared to treat it as settled. | He treats the word as genuinely debated. Egyptian explanations are possible and may fit the Joseph context, but he does not present "kneel" or any other derivation as certain enough to close the question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=3435" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:15</a> | Can a case be made that the flood was local while still functionally worldwide? | He thought a purely local reading runs into real problems, especially the ark's animal cargo, even if a limited-human-world perspective explains part of the story. | He acknowledges that ancient perspective can affect how "world" language is read. Still, he says the ark and animals create problems for a merely local flood model, so the explanation must handle more than human geography. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=3654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:54</a> | What are his thoughts on the recent announced finding about Old Kingdom mummification? | He called it an exciting and important discovery because it pushes clear evidence for mummification back to the Old Kingdom and shows how positive evidence can change Egyptology. | He sees the discovery as important because it revises what can be said from positive evidence. If Old Kingdom mummification is more clearly attested, then the history of Egyptian funerary practice must be adjusted earlier rather than left at older assumptions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=3923" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:23</a> | Was witchcraft taboo in Mesopotamia while wizardry was acceptable? | He said the distinction is blurry. Some private or necromantic practices were forbidden, but public, priestly, and divinatory magic were widely practiced. | He explains that Mesopotamian categories do not map neatly onto modern labels. Certain harmful, private, or necromantic practices could be condemned, while official divination and ritual magic were central parts of learned priestly life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=4119" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:39</a> | Is the Potiphar story basically the same as the Tale of Two Brothers? | Only vaguely. He said there are some parallels and possible name echoes, but the legal and social settings are quite different. | He recognizes a broad motif of false accusation or sexual accusation, and possibly some suggestive names. But he says the stories differ enough in setting, law, and social context that Genesis should not be treated as a simple copy of the Egyptian tale. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=4420" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:40</a> | Was it normal in the ancient Near East to bury families together, and why was Leah buried with the patriarchs instead of Rachel? | Yes, family tombs were common. He said Leah's burial reflects her role in the ancestral family tomb structure. | He says family tombs were normal and socially meaningful. Leah's placement with the patriarchs reflects her status within the ancestral burial structure, while Rachel's separate burial is explained by the narrative circumstances of her death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=4630" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:10</a> | Should Nectanebo II or Cleopatra VII be considered the last pharaoh? | He said no; Cleopatra was not even the last Ptolemaic ruler, Roman emperors also took the title, and identifying the final pharaoh becomes ambiguous in the Christian period. | He argues that "last pharaoh" depends on how the title is defined. Native rule, Ptolemaic rule, Roman imperial titulary, and later Christian-era changes each produce different answers, so neither Nectanebo II nor Cleopatra VII is a simple final endpoint. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=4804" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:04</a> | Did Ramesses II's firstborn son die from a head injury? | He said there is no evidence for that and no Egyptian record of the cause of death. | He rejects the specific head-injury claim because the evidence is absent. The death of Ramesses II's firstborn can be discussed historically, but the cause of death is not preserved in the Egyptian record. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=4913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:53</a> | Was the witch of Endor an anomaly? | No. He said the surrounding nations practiced magic openly, and Saul had to cross out of Israelite-controlled territory to seek her. | He says the episode is unusual within Israelite law but not unusual in the broader region. Saul's journey outside normal Israelite control makes sense because surrounding peoples practiced forms of magic and necromancy more openly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=5384" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:44</a> | Did the Pharaoh of the Exodus drown with his army, or only the army? | He said the text points to the destruction of the chariot force and army, not to Pharaoh himself drowning. | He reads the biblical wording as emphasizing the destruction of Pharaoh's military force. He does not think the text requires Pharaoh personally to have died in the sea, even though his army and chariots are portrayed as overwhelmed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=5846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:26</a> | Were pyramids famous around the world during the pyramid age itself? | Not nearly as early or as widely as people assume. He said they become clearly famous much later, especially from around 700 BCE onward. | He says pyramid fame is not automatic in the Old Kingdom itself. The pyramids become much more clearly known internationally in later periods, especially when Greek and Near Eastern awareness of Egypt expands. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=6109" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:49</a> | Did the name Jesus come from "hail Zeus"? | No. He called that an untenable pseudo-etymology and said the name is simply the Greek/Hellenized form of Yeshua. | He rejects the "hail Zeus" claim as a false etymology based on sound resemblance rather than language history. Jesus comes through Greek from the Hebrew or Aramaic name Yeshua, not from a reference to Zeus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DVV7DMRiBL4?t=6704" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:44</a> | How should Christians read the church fathers and patristic literature? | He said they should be read in context as early witnesses after the apostles, not as proof-texts detached from their historical setting. | He recommends reading patristic writers as historically situated early Christian witnesses. They are valuable but should not be lifted out of context or treated as automatic final authorities apart from Scripture and historical interpretation. |
